reasoning:
The 180-day average of SOFR on June 8, 2025 will be approximately 4.393. Because the daily SOFR is currently much lower (around 4.30%, following rate cuts in late 2024 and early 2025), replacing older, higher SOFR days (from Dec 2024/Jan 2025) with current lower rates mechanically forces the 180-day average downward by about 0.0015 to 0.002 per day. This mathematical downtrend is guaranteed to continue through at least mid-to-late June until all pre-cut higher rates are purged from the 180-day moving window. 

As a result:
- By June 15 and July 8, the average will deterministically be much lower than the June 8 level. 
- To resolve YES for Sept 6 or Dec 5, the Fed would need to initiate a series of unexpected rate hikes to bring the daily SOFR significantly above 4.40%, and hold it there long enough to drag the 180-day average back up. 
- CME FedWatch data shows markets overwhelmingly expect further rate cuts or holds in 2025, pricing a rate hike at <5% probability. Even if a surprise hike occurs, the 180-day window heavily dilutes its short-term impact. Thus, the likelihood of the average recovering to the June 8 mark is minimal across all horizons, with only slight uncertainty for December if inflation unexpectedly surges.
